description: Test vocab_size=896 to check if fewer tokens reduces dead vocab fraction enough to offset slightly worse compression

decision.headline: Vocab 896 worse: compression loss outweighs savings
decision.reasoning: Score regressed from 0.4232 to 0.4377 (+0.0145), a significant worsening. While dead vocab dropped from 14.2% to 0.3% and artifact budget decreased by ~18KB, the compression ratio worsened from 0.411 to 0.430 tokens_per_byte (+4.5% more tokens). The compression penalty far outweighs the modest parameter savings of 65K params (~18KB artifact). The baseline at 1024 vocab with 14% dead tokens is still superior overall.
decision.frontier_update: Shrinking vocab below 1024 is counterproductive — the compression loss dominates. Dead vocab at 14% is acceptable at this scale.
decision.next_direction: Try vocab_size=1152 or 1280 to see if slightly larger vocab improves compression enough to justify the extra parameters

description: Test vocab_size=1152, slightly above baseline 1024, to see if modest vocab increase improves compression enough to justify extra embedding cost

decision.headline: vocab 1152 beats baseline by 1.5% on composite score
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.4232 to 0.4167, a meaningful 1.5% reduction. tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.4113 to 0.4064 (1.2% fewer tokens), dead vocab collapsed from 14.2% to 0.35%, and artifact budget fraction only rose from 0.91% to 1.02%. The 65K extra params cost is modest relative to the compression and dead-vocab gains.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=1152 at score 0.4167; larger-than-1024 vocabs are viable
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=1280 and 1408 to find the sweet spot before embedding cost dominates

description: Test vocab_size=1280 to find sweet spot between 1152 and 1536 where compression gains vs embedding cost

decision.headline: vocab 1280 beats frontier by 1.7%, new best composite score
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.4167 to 0.4095, a 1.7% reduction. tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.4064 to 0.3980 (2% fewer tokens), dead_vocab_frac stayed negligible at 0.3%, and artifact budget fraction only rose from 1.02% to 1.14% — the compression gain clearly outweighs the extra embedding cost. This confirms the trend that larger vocabs are still net-positive at this range.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=1280 at score 0.4095; the compression-vs-embedding tradeoff is still favorable above 1152
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=1408 to see if the trend continues or if embedding cost starts dominating

description: Test vocab_size=1408 as suggested by frontier analysis - continue the upward trend from 1280 to see if compression gains still outpace embedding cost

decision.headline: vocab 1408 beats frontier by 1.9%, compression gains still outpacing embedding cost
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.4095 to 0.4016, a 1.9% reduction (lower is better). tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.398 to 0.389 while artifact budget fraction only grew from 1.14% to 1.25%. Dead vocab remains negligible at 0.35%. The compression-vs-embedding tradeoff is still clearly favorable at this vocab size.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=1408 at score 0.4016; the trend from 1024→1152→1280→1408 shows diminishing but still positive returns — each 128-token step yields smaller score gains (baseline→0.4095→0.4016)
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=1536 to find where the curve flattens, and consider a parallel probe at 1792 or 2048 to bracket the inflection point faster

description: Test vocab_size=1536, next step up from 1408 to find where diminishing returns flatten

decision.headline: vocab 1536 beats frontier by 1.5%, compression gains still outpacing embedding cost
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.4016 to 0.3954, a 1.5% gain. tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.3890 to 0.3817 while artifact budget fraction only rose from 1.25% to 1.37%. Dead vocab remains negligible at 0.33%. The trend 1024→1152→1280→1408→1536 continues to show positive returns, though the per-step gain is shrinking (1.9% last step vs 1.5% this step).
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=1536 at score 0.3954; the gain curve is flattening but still clearly positive — each 128-token step costs ~65KB artifact budget but saves ~350K tokens across the corpus
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=1792 to skip ahead and bracket the inflection point faster, since 128-token steps are slow and returns are still positive

description: Test vocab_size=1792 to bracket the inflection point where artifact cost overtakes compression gains

decision.headline: vocab 1792 beats frontier by 2.6%, compression gains still outpacing embedding cost
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.3954 to 0.3853, a 2.6% reduction. tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.3817 to 0.3693 (3.2% fewer tokens) while artifact budget fraction only grew from 1.37% to 1.59%. Dead vocab remains negligible at 0.28%. The compression-to-cost tradeoff is still clearly favorable.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=1792 at score 0.3853; the gain from 1536→1792 (256 tokens, score delta -0.0101) is comparable per-token to the 1024→1536 step, suggesting diminishing returns haven't hit hard yet but the curve is starting to flatten
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=2304 to take a larger step and find where returns inflect sharply, since 256-token steps are still clearly positive

description: Test vocab_size=2304 to find where diminishing returns inflect, as suggested by frontier analysis — the 256-token steps from 1024→1792 have been consistently positive

decision.headline: vocab 2304 beats frontier by 3.4%, compression still outpacing embedding cost
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.3853 to 0.3722, a delta of -0.0131 (3.4% relative improvement). The 1792→2304 step (+512 tokens) yielded a larger absolute gain than the 1536→1792 step (-0.0101 for +256 tokens), meaning per-token returns have not yet diminished — the curve is still concave. Artifact budget fraction rose from 1.6% to 2.0%, well within tolerance, and dead vocab remains negligible at 0.3%.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=2304 at score 0.3722; the gain per additional token is holding steady (~0.000025 per token), indicating the inflection point has not been reached yet and larger steps are still warranted
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=3072 to continue probing for the inflection point with another +768 step, since returns remain linear

description: Test vocab_size=3072 to continue probing for the inflection point where marginal compression gains no longer justify embedding cost, following the linear trend observed from 1792→2304

decision.headline: vocab 3072 beats frontier by 3.1%, compression gains still outpacing embedding cost
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.3722 to 0.3606 (lower is better), a 3.1% gain. tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.3516 to 0.3333 while artifact budget fraction only rose from 2.05% to 2.73%. Dead vocab remains negligible at 0.23%. The marginal gain per +768 tokens (~0.0116 score improvement) is comparable to the previous step (0.0131), confirming we haven't hit the inflection point yet.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=3072 at score 0.3606; marginal returns per token are slightly decelerating (0.0116 vs 0.0131 for the prior step) but still strongly positive, suggesting the inflection point is approaching but not yet reached
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=4096 with a larger +1024 step to find whether returns flatten at higher vocab sizes

description: Test vocab_size=4096 to find whether compression returns flatten at higher vocab sizes

decision.headline: vocab 4096 beats frontier by 2.3%, but marginal returns are clearly decelerating
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.3606 to 0.3525, a meaningful 2.3% gain. Tokens_per_byte dropped from 0.333 to 0.316 (5.2% fewer tokens), while artifact budget fraction rose from 2.7% to 3.6% — still well within budget. However, the marginal score improvement per +1024 vocab tokens dropped from 0.0116 (2048→3072) to 0.0081 (3072→4096), confirming diminishing returns are accelerating. Dead vocab remains negligible at 0.2%.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=4096 at score 0.3525; marginal gain per +1024 tokens fell to 0.0081 (from 0.0116), signaling we are approaching the inflection point where embedding cost will overtake compression benefit — likely around 6K-8K vocab
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=6144 with a +2048 step to locate the inflection point where embedding overhead overtakes compression gains

description: Test vocab_size=6144 to locate the inflection point where embedding overhead overtakes compression gains, using a +2048 step from the current best at 4096

decision.headline: vocab 6144 beats frontier by 1.1%, compression gains still positive but decelerating sharply
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 0.3525 to 0.3488, a 1.1% gain. The marginal gain per +1024 tokens dropped further: +2048 tokens yielded only 0.0037 score improvement (0.0019 per +1024), down from 0.0081 per +1024 at the 4096 step. Artifact budget fraction rose to 5.5% (from 3.6%), meaning embedding cost is growing linearly while compression returns are clearly diminishing. We are approaching but have not yet crossed the inflection point.
decision.frontier_update: new best is vocab_size=6144 at score 0.3488; marginal gain per +1024 tokens collapsed to 0.0019 (from 0.0081), confirming we are very near the inflection point — the next +2048 step will likely cross it
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=8192 to confirm the inflection point, then if it regresses, bisect between 6144-8192 to find the optimal vocab size

description: Test vocab_size=8192 to confirm inflection point where artifact cost overtakes compression gains

decision.headline: vocab 8192 regresses score to 0.3531 vs frontier 0.3488 — inflection point confirmed, artifact cost dominates
decision.reasoning: Vocab 8192 reduces tokens_per_byte by 4.7% (0.294→0.280) but increases artifact budget fraction from 5.5% to 7.3%, a +1.8pp jump. The net score regresses from 0.3488 to 0.3531 (+0.0043), confirming that compression gains no longer outpace the vocab-linked parameter cost. The inflection point lies between 6144 and 8192. Dead vocab fraction remains low at 0.45%, so the regression is purely from artifact overhead.
decision.frontier_update: vocab 6144 remains the frontier; the inflection point is confirmed between 6144 and 8192 — larger vocabs are now net negative
decision.next_direction: bisect at vocab_size=7168 (midpoint of 6144-8192) to find the exact optimal vocab size in this range

description: Bisect between 6144 (best, score 0.3488) and 8192 (regressed, score 0.3531) to find optimal vocab size near the inflection point

decision.headline: vocab 7168 confirms inflection: score 0.3505 is worse than 6144's 0.3488
decision.reasoning: The bisect at vocab_size=7168 scored 0.3505, which is worse than the frontier at 6144 (0.3488). While tokens_per_byte improved slightly (0.287 vs 0.294), the additional 1024 vocab tokens cost ~146K more artifact bytes without enough compression gain to offset. This confirms 6144 is on the correct side of the inflection point, with the optimum lying at or below 6144.
decision.frontier_update: inflection point is now bracketed: 6144 (0.3488) < optimal <= 7168 (0.3505). The optimum is at or very near 6144. Upward vocab expansion is exhausted.
decision.next_direction: bisect downward: test vocab_size=5632 (between 5120 and 6144) to check if 6144 is truly optimal or if a slightly smaller vocab scores better

description: Bisect between 4096 and 6144: test vocab_size=5120 to find if the optimum is closer to 4096 or 6144

The smaller vocab saves ~146K artifact bytes but gets worse compression (0.3036 vs 0.2941 tokens_per_byte), and the net score is 0.0005 worse. This confirms the optimum is at or above 6144, not between 4096-6144.
decision.frontier_update: 5120 is ruled out; the optimum lies at 6144 or higher. The original plan to test 8192 to find the inflection point remains the right next step.
decision.next_direction: Test vocab_size=8192 to determine if gains continue above 6144 or if we've already passed the inflection point.

description: Bisect between 5120 and 6144 to find if the optimum is closer to 6144 or between them

The bisection confirms that 5632 tokens compresses less effectively (0.2986 vs 0.2941 tokens/byte) and the small artifact savings (~73KB less) don't compensate for the worse compression. This confirms 6144 is better than anything below it in the 5120-6144 range.
decision.frontier_update: the optimum is at or above 6144, not between 5120 and 6144 — the left side of the bisection is now closed
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=7168 (bisect 6144-8192) to find the true inflection point on the right side

description: Test larger tokenizer training sample at best vocab size 6144 to improve merge quality

decision.headline: Larger training sample at vocab 6144 produces identical tokenizer — no improvement
decision.reasoning: Experiment 0015 tested input_sentence_size=500000 at vocab_size=6144 but achieved an identical score of 0.3488 and identical metrics across the board (same tokens_per_byte, same dead_vocab_frac, same unique_tokens_used=6129). The larger training sample did not improve merge quality at all. This is a no-op result.
decision.frontier_update: Training sample size is not a lever at this vocab size — the 9770-chunk default already saturates merge quality for vocab 6144. Focus remains on vocab size exploration.
decision.next_direction: Test vocab_size=8192 to confirm the inflection point as previously planned, then bisect between 6144-8192 if it regresses.

description: Try unigram model type at optimal vocab_size=6144 to see if different segmentation algorithm beats BPE frontier

decision.headline: unigram at 6144 loses to BPE by 2.1% — BPE remains superior segmentation
decision.reasoning: Unigram model at vocab_size=6144 scores 0.3561 vs BPE frontier of 0.3488, a clear regression of +0.0073. It produces more tokens (14.65M vs 14.29M, i.e. worse compression at 0.3014 vs 0.2941 tokens_per_byte) while consuming the same vocab budget. The only marginal win is slightly fewer dead vocab tokens (0.065% vs 0.24%), which is negligible. BPE is strictly better here.
decision.frontier_update: unigram segmentation ruled out at the optimal vocab size — BPE confirmed as the right model type for this corpus and scale
decision.next_direction: test vocab_size=8192 BPE to confirm the inflection point, then bisect 6144-8192 if it regresses

decision.next_direction: bisect downward: try vocab_size=5632 to verify 6144 is truly optimal and not just the best power-of-1024 tested

decision.next_direction: Try vocab_size=6144-6400 range with different model_type (unigram) or byte_fallback settings to squeeze more compression from the same artifact budget.

decision.headline: Lower character_coverage=0.995 hurts compression vs 0.999 at same vocab_size=6400
decision.reasoning: Experiment 0033 scores 0.34906 vs the frontier's 0.34832, a regression of 0.00073 (0.21% worse). Lowering character_coverage from 0.999 to 0.995 increased tokens_per_byte from 0.29132 to 0.29207, meaning worse compression. The hypothesis that freeing rare-character slots would yield better BPE merges did not pay off — the rare characters were apparently useful.
decision.frontier_update: character_coverage=0.999 remains optimal at vocab_size=6400; reducing coverage hurts rather than helps. The search space for coverage tuning below 0.999 is unlikely to be productive.
decision.next_direction: Test vocab_size=7168 or 8192 at character_coverage=0.999 to find where artifact cost overtakes compression gains.

description: Test vocab_size=6400 BPE with 1M input sentences to see if more training data improves merge quality beyond the 500K baseline that produced the current frontier

decision.headline: 1M input sentences produces identical tokenizer to 500K baseline — no improvement
decision.reasoning: The score is exactly 0.3483178826428708, identical to the frontier (experiment 0022). Every metric matches: same tokens_per_byte (0.2913), same unique_tokens_used (6380), same dead_vocab_frac (0.003125). Doubling the training sentences from 500K to 1M produced byte-identical merge quality, meaning 500K sentences already saturates the BPE training for vocab_size=6400.
decision.frontier_update: SentencePiece BPE training data volume is saturated at 500K sentences for this vocab size; no need to explore higher input_sentence_size values
decision.next_direction: Test vocab_size=7168 or 8192 to find where artifact cost penalty overtakes compression benefit
